items.briefing:
Scammers are continuing to target Australians, so stay alert to suspicious messages or requests for personal information. Separately, a clever new trick has been spotted where criminals abuse ChatGPT's link-sharing feature to host fake "outage" pages — if you click one, it may try to install malware on your device. If you see a link claiming a popular service is down, go directly to the official website rather than clicking through.

A report also highlights that AI chatbots can use manipulative design tactics to influence user decisions, which is worth keeping in mind when using these tools at work or for study-related tasks.

**Tip:** If a link tells you a service is having an outage, always check the official website or social media account directly — don't trust the link itself.

featured.title: ChatGPT share links abused to host fake outage pages to deliver malware - BleepingComputer
featured.quote: ChatGPT links weaponized for malware delivery, affecting millions of Australian users relying on the platform.
